title: Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Energy intensity represents [primary energy](#dod:primaryenergy) consumption, using the [substitution method](#dod:substitutionmethod), per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Energy intensity vs. GDP per capita, 2022

Energy intensity represents primary energy consumption, using the substitution method, per unit of gross domestic product (GDP). GDP is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between energy intensity and GDP per capita for various countries in 2022, with GDP data expressed in international-$ at 2011 prices. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale ranging from $1,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis measures energy intensity in kilowatt-hours per dollar from 0 kWh to over 4 kWh. Points are color-coded by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A distinct cluster of nations with low GDP per capita, such as Burundi, Liberia, and Chad, occupies the bottom-left corner, showing values below $2,000 and energy intensity generally under 1 kWh. In contrast, high-income economies like Norway, Denmark, and Australia appear on the far right with GDP per capita exceeding $50,000 and energy intensity typically between 1 and 2 kWh. Several countries deviate significantly from this general trend; Turkmenistan, North Korea, and Bahrain exhibit very high energy intensity above 4 kWh despite varying GDP levels. Venezuela and Zimbabwe also show elevated energy intensity between 2 and 4.5 kWh with GDP per capita roughly between $2,000 and $6,000. Meanwhile, countries like Qatar and Kuwait maintain high GDP per capita above $50,000 with moderate energy intensity around 1.5 to 2.5 kWh. The distribution suggests that while many wealthy nations cluster in a specific efficiency band, outliers exist across the entire economic spectrum.
